subject.title: English A Lang & Lit
subject.description:
**IB English A Language & Literature** is a **Group 1** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **the study of language and literature through a wide variety of texts and media from different periods, styles, and cultures**, with a strong focus on **critical analysis of both literary and non-literary texts, examining how language choices create meaning and effect in different contexts**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2021**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| **Assessment** | **Format** | **SL** | **HL** | **Weighting** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Paper 1** | Text analysis | 1h 15m | 2h 15m | 35% (SL), 25% (HL) |
| **Paper 2** | Comparative essay | 1h 45m | 1h 45m | 35% (SL), 25% (HL)  |
| **HL Essay** | Written assignment | N/A | 1200-1500 words | 25% (HL only) |
| **Individual Oral** | Oral presentation | 15 minutes | 15 minutes | 30% (SL), 20% (HL) |

strengths: The research question is stated clearly, links both albums and critical reception, and guides the analysis effectively.
Contextualisation of historical, social, and feminist movements demonstrates informed selection of relevant sources.
Methodology section outlines a coherent multi-method qualitative approach (lyric close readings, interviews, reviews).
The topic is communicated accurately and with clear purpose through the introduction and background definitions.
weaknesses: Background discussion occasionally repeats ideas and could be tightened to maintain razor-sharp focus.
Justification for selecting only two songs per album is thin and needs more explicit criteria.
At times the essay digresses into contextual description without tightly anchoring to the research question.
Minor prolix phrasing slightly blurs the focus in places.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student consistently demonstrates a clear link between the research question, context, and methodology (strands in the 3â4 band), with one strand approaching top-band performance in topic communication (band 5). Overall, the focus is generally maintained but not fully sharpened, warranting 4 out of 6.

strengths: Key concepts (trauma, dysfunctional relationships) are defined with appropriate scholarly citations.
Good awareness of lyrical content, socio-cultural context, and contemporary criticism.
Mostly accurate and consistent use of subject-specific terminology (metaphor, symbolism, diction).
weaknesses: Several claims (e.g., biographical details) lack reputable academic citation.
Reliance on journalistic and popular sources limits academic rigour and exhaustiveness.
Synthesis of secondary scholarship with primary lyric analysis is intermittent.
Occasional imprecision in terminology (e.g., conflating metaphor and symbolism).
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The work shows good knowledge of the topic and generally accurate use of terminology, placing it solidly in the 3â4 band. Gaps in academic sourcing and intermittently superficial synthesis prevent a higher mark, supporting a score of 4 out of 6.

strengths: Bibliography includes a broad range of primary lyrics, scholarly articles, interviews and music-press reviews, showing substantial research relevance.
Close readings of âShades of Cool,â âHe Hit Meâ¦,â and simile in âUltraviolenceâ reveal perceptive thematic insights.
Incorporation of a social-work researcherâs perspective enriches audience-reception analysis.
Strong identification of literary devices (repetition, imagery) with well-supported conclusions.
Comparative synthesis across both albums demonstrates organisational clarity and connection to the research question.
weaknesses: Heavy reliance on popular blogs and entertainment sites weakens scholarly rigour.
Analysis sometimes drifts into summary of lyrics and reviews rather than deeper interrogation.
Evaluation of criticsâ perspectives lacks sustained critical weighing of their significance.
Limited engagement with alternative interpretations or counter-arguments.
A few claims throughout lack explicit citation, undermining evidence support.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Research quality sits at the lower end of the 7â9 band, while analysis depth and evaluation are solidly in the 4â6 band. Overall, the student presents good critical thinking with mostly supported conclusions and relevant discussion, meriting 9 out of 12.

strengths: The essay follows the required EE structure with a clear title page, table of contents, logical sections, conclusion and works-cited list.
Section and subsection headings are clearly styled, aiding reader navigation of complex analytic content.
Page numbering, word count, and title placement meet IB requirements and enhance professional presentation.
weaknesses: Citation style fluctuates between APA and MLA conventions, detracting from polish.
Unclear labels (e.g., âCategory 1â) and redundant section titles reduce clarity.
Minor typographic spacing issues and occasional layout inconsistencies are present.
Some headings overlap in scope, suggesting a need to streamline the organisational framework.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Structure meets all EE requirements (band 4) but formatting inconsistencies and minor redundancies limit the overall polish. The combination of top-band organisation and sub-top-band formatting supports a score of 3 out of 4.

strandSelections.reasoning:
The student outlines a qualitative content-analysis methodology, identifies close reading of selected lyrics, artist interviews, critical reviews, and contextual research. This constitutes a mostly complete and relevant set of methods and sources, showing some informed choices.

Nonetheless, justification for limiting the lyric study to only two songs per album is thin, and the criteria for source selection (especially blogs and popular journalism) is not critically discussed. The linkage between method and research question is therefore serviceable rather than fully convincing, placing the work in the upper end of the 3-4 band.

The formatting generally adheres to IB guidelines: a word count is provided; a clear, numbered table of contents is present; headings are consistently styled; and in-text citations plus a works-cited list are included. Page divisions are evident in the submission extract, suggesting pagination.

Minor inconsistencies in citation style (e.g., mixed use of MLA/APA conventions) and occasional typographic spacing issues detract slightly from professional polish, so a solid but not perfect score of 3 within the top band (3-4) is appropriate.

The bibliography lists a variety of primary lyrics, music-press reviews, scholarly articles, and interviews, indicating a broad research effort. Most sources are directly pertinent to the exploration of trauma, romanticization, and cultural reception, and are woven into the discussion with reasonable frequency.

Some reliance on popular media (blogs, entertainment sites) weakens academic rigour, and a few claims lack explicit citation. Nevertheless, the overall range and relevance of research are solid and mostly well connected to the research question, justifying placement at the lower end of the 7-9 band.

The student conveys the topicâthe romanticization of trauma in Fiona Appleâs Tidal and Lana Del Reyâs Ultraviolenceâclearly from the title, research question, and introduction. The background paragraph defines key terms (trauma, dysfunctional relationships) and explains the cultural significance of examining these portrayals, giving the reader a solid sense of purpose. 

Minor weaknesses appear in the occasional prolix phrasing and slight repetition, which at times blur the razor-sharpness of focus; nevertheless, the overarching line of inquiry remains easy to follow throughout the essay. These small lapses prevent the work from reaching the absolute top of the band, but overall communication of topic and purpose is accurate and effective.

The discussion moves from individual song analysis to broader cultural contexts and concludes with a comparative synthesis, showing an organised line of argument. The student recognises differing critical and audience responses and attempts to evaluate their significance.

However, evaluative moments tend to be brief and sometimes rely on assertion rather than sustained critical weighing of perspectives. Nuanced implications for the field of popular-music studies are hinted at but not fully developed. This places the work comfortably in the middle of the 4-6 band.

The student provides sustained close readings of four songs, identifying literary devices and linking them to themes of guilt, power, and romanticization. Comparative observations between Apple and Del Rey reveal some thoughtful insight, and conclusions are generally supported by lyrical evidence and secondary commentary.

Nonetheless, analysis occasionally drifts into lengthy paraphrase or summary of lyrics and reviews, with limited interrogation of alternative interpretations or counter-arguments. The reasoning is therefore good but not consistently penetrating, fitting mid-to-high within the 4-6 band.

The essay follows the required EE structure: title page with word count, abstract omitted as per current guide, clear table of contents, sequentially numbered sections, conclusion, and works-cited list. Transitions between sections are logical, enabling reader navigation.

While some sections (e.g., âRomanticization of Trauma Across Both Albumsâ and âThematic Analysisâ) risk overlap, overall organisation supports the argument effectively. Hence the structure meets all requirements and merits the top band, with minor redundancies keeping the score at 4 rather than perfect.

Across the analysis sections the student demonstrates good awareness of each albumâs lyrical content, socio-cultural context, and contemporary criticism. References to third- and fourth-wave feminism, specific reviews, and selected scholarly articles indicate a reasonable command of the topic.

Yet a number of the sources are journalistic or popular rather than academic, and the synthesis of secondary material with primary lyric analysis is intermittent. This results in a sound but not fully exhaustive demonstration of knowledge, meriting a score in the 3-4 band.

The essay employs literary-critical terminology such as âmetaphor,â âsymbolism,â âjuxtaposition,â and âdictionâ with general accuracy, and these terms are usually applied correctly to specific lyric excerpts. The student also references cultural concepts like third- and fourth-wave feminism appropriately.

There are, however, moments of imprecision (e.g., conflating metaphor and symbolism, or using âromanticizationâ without clearly delimiting its critical nuance). Overall, terminology usage is mostly accurate and purposeful, aligning with the descriptors for the 3-4 band.

The research question is explicitly stated, addresses both albums, and links to critical reception, demonstrating clear intent. It is largely appropriate for an English A: Language & Literature EE and is referred to in subsequent sections. 

However, by combining two substantial albums and a dual focus on both representation and reception, the question risks breadth over depth; the essay occasionally digresses into contextual description without tightly anchoring every section to the question. Thus, while the focus is generally maintained, it is only partially sharpened, fitting solidly within the 3-4 band.

annotations.comment: The Table of Contents is well-structured and styled, enabling easy navigation through the sections of the essay.
The research question is stated clearly and connects both albums and eras coherently, providing a focused inquiry that guides the subsequent analysis effectively.
The inclusion of a precise word count at the start of the essay meets IB formatting requirements and demonstrates attention to guidelines.
The title is presented prominently, immediately conveying the topic and scope of the investigation to the reader.
The label âCategory 1â is unclear to readers and does not align with standard EE conventions; consider removing or replacing it with a clear subtitle.
The shift from comparative to thematic analysis headings introduces minor redundancy; consider streamlining section titles for clarity.
Incorporating a social-work researcherâs perspective enriches the analysis of audience reception with an academic dimension.
Including a contextual analysis of historical, social, and feminist movements demonstrates informed selection of relevant contextual sources.
The sectional heading âThe Romanticization of Trauma Across Both Albumsâ is clearly styled and signals an effective organizational structure.
The close reading of âCriminalâ effectively identifies imagery but relies heavily on summary; deepen analysis by exploring the lyricâs broader cultural resonances.
The claim that Apple endured rape at age 12 is critical but uncited; include a reputable biographical source or scholar to substantiate this context.
The analysis of âShades of Coolâ thoughtfully links lyrical imagery to cycles of self-destruction, demonstrating perceptive thematic insight.
The interpretation of âHe hit me and it felt like a kissâ incisively captures the psychological distortion of abuse as twisted affection.
The repeated-lines analysis in âCriminalâ persuasively demonstrates how repetition conveys guilt and powerlessness in the lyricâs confessional voice.
The extended reading of the simile in âUltraviolenceâ effectively highlights the normalization and romanticization of abuse through linguistic contrast.
The subsection âTurmoil and Self-Perceptionâ is clearly signposted, aiding reader navigation of complex analytic content.
The introduction defines key concepts (trauma, dysfunctional relationships) with an appropriate scholarly citation, demonstrating solid subject knowledge.
The background discussion, while descriptive, occasionally repeats ideas about portrayal and debate; tightening prose will sharpen the essay's focus.
The methodology section outlines qualitative content-analysis, lyric close readings, interviews, and secondary reviews, showing a coherent multi-method approach.
The overview of third-wave feminism is accurate but could be more tightly linked back to specific lyrical examples to enhance relevance.
The sentence describing the title track ends abruptly and appears incomplete; ensure all sentences are fully rendered in the final draft.
The works-cited list continues to mix styles and lacks clear authorâdate ordering; reorganise entries alphabetically and standardise citation elements.
Consistent formatting of section titles and page numbers in the contents reflects careful attention to layout.
Justification for selecting only two songs per album is thin; explicitly state selection criteria to strengthen methodological rigour.
The depiction of Appleâs âunder the wavesâ imagery insightfully reads the lyricâs representation of detachment as both refuge and risk.
The resilience discussion is insightful but would benefit from integrating secondary scholarship to deepen contextual support.
Citation style fluctuates between APA and MLA conventions in this section; adopt a single consistent style for professional polish.
Reporting criticsâ views provides context but lacks evaluative depth; add critical commentary to weigh the significance of these perspectives.
The contextualisation of cultural and social movements demonstrates a clear connection between the research question and broader historical frameworks.
The opening note on fourth-wave authenticity reiterates earlier points; consider consolidating cultural context discussions to avoid redundancy.
The clear labelling of the conclusion section enhances structural coherence and signals closure effectively.
Bibliographic entries display inconsistent indentation and punctuation; apply a uniform citation format and hanging-indent style.
